The Hamnix Project

Hamnix is a native operating system written in the Adder language. It follows a Plan 9 ethos: everything is a file server, and resources are controlled by writing to control files rather than by system calls.

This article is a plain page with no author stylesheet at all, so it shows exactly what the built-in default styling looks like. A good browser makes even an unstyled document readable: comfortable margins, a sensible measure, and a clear heading hierarchy.

Design goals

The system aims to be a credible Unix competitor while keeping the design clean. The following goals drive the work:

  • Ship a real distribution that can consume Debian repositories.
  • Keep the native kernel Plan 9-shaped, with hardware reached through a thin shim layer.
  • Provide a desktop environment built on display-list scene files.

Bringing a release together happens in a fixed order:

  1. Cut a branch and freeze the feature set.
  2. Run the full battery and fix every regression.
  3. Tag the image and publish the signed index.

Where things stand

The table below sketches how a few subsystems compare with a Linux baseline; native means no shim is involved.

SubsystemStatusNative
Filesystemext4 read/writeyes
Graphicssoftware rasteryes
Wi-Fivia .ko shimno

A note on typography

Read the classic advice from this reference on line length. Lines that stretch edge to edge are tiring; a narrower column keeps the eye anchored and makes long articles pleasant.

Anything that can go wrong will go wrong, but a good default stylesheet keeps a plain page looking intentional anyway.

Example code

Preformatted and inline code keep a monospace look:

fn main() {
    print("hello, hamnix");
}

Footer text after a horizontal rule. Visit the local server for more.
